text.content: 1.1  Opening Frame  [s1_1_opening_frame]

rich_text.plain_text: 1.1  Opening Frame  [s1_1_opening_frame]

text.content: "You've been using the {commutative property} to solve problems, with arrays, without arrays, in all kinds of situations. Let's step back and connect what you discovered."

rich_text.plain_text: "You've been using the {commutative property} to solve problems, with arrays, without arrays, in all kinds of situations. Let's step back and connect what you discovered."

text.content: "Two ways to show the {commutative property} works for multiplication. On the left, describing rows versus describing columns. On the right, rotation. What's the SAME about both?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Two ways to show the {commutative property} works for multiplication. On the left, describing rows versus describing columns. On the right, rotation. What's the SAME about both?"

text.content:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["Same dots, nothing is added or removed", "Both methods give the same expression", "Both use columns only"]
"What do both proofs have in common?"

rich_text.plain_text:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["Same dots, nothing is added or removed", "Both methods give the same expression", "Both use columns only"]
"What do both proofs have in common?"

text.content: ✅ Student selected A, correct answer. Identified the structural anchor.

rich_text.plain_text: ✅ Student selected A, correct answer. Identified the structural anchor.

text.content: "Right. Same dots. That's the anchor. Whether you read it a different way or turn it, no dots change. Same dots means same total. Always."

rich_text.plain_text: "Right. Same dots. That's the anchor. Whether you read it a different way or turn it, no dots change. Same dots means same total. Always."

text.content: ❌ Student selected B: focused on the equation result rather than the structural anchor.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected B: focused on the equation result rather than the structural anchor.

text.content: Left array highlights as a complete set.

rich_text.plain_text: Left array highlights as a complete set.

text.content: Right array highlights as a complete set.

rich_text.plain_text: Right array highlights as a complete set.

text.content: "That's not it. Each method actually gives TWO different expressions: 4 × 5 and 5 × 4. What stayed exactly the same in both methods?"

rich_text.plain_text: "That's not it. Each method actually gives TWO different expressions: 4 × 5 and 5 × 4. What stayed exactly the same in both methods?"

text.content: ❌ Student selected C: incorrect focus on method detail rather than structural invariant.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected C: incorrect focus on method detail rather than structural invariant.

text.content: Left array: rows flash briefly to show row method was also used.

rich_text.plain_text: Left array: rows flash briefly to show row method was also used.

text.content: "Actually, both involve columns AND rows. In either method, what didn't change?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Actually, both involve columns AND rows. In either method, what didn't change?"

text.content: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

text.content: Left array: all dots pulse to emphasize total count remains 20.

rich_text.plain_text: Left array: all dots pulse to emphasize total count remains 20.

text.content: Right array: all dots pulse to emphasize total count remains 20.

rich_text.plain_text: Right array: all dots pulse to emphasize total count remains 20.

text.content: "Here's what I notice about these two methods. On the left, we looked at the same dots a different way, first by rows, then by columns. On the right, we turned the same dots. But in both cases, the dots themselves never changed. No dots added, no dots removed. Same dots, same total. That's the anchor."

rich_text.plain_text: "Here's what I notice about these two methods. On the left, we looked at the same dots a different way, first by rows, then by columns. On the right, we turned the same dots. But in both cases, the dots themselves never changed. No dots added, no dots removed. Same dots, same total. That's the anchor."

text.content: 2.1  Generalization Always Works  [s2_1_generalization_always_works]

rich_text.plain_text: 2.1  Generalization Always Works  [s2_1_generalization_always_works]

text.content: "Here's one you haven't seen as an array today. 9 times 7 is 63. What's 7 times 9?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Here's one you haven't seen as an array today. 9 times 7 is 63. What's 7 times 9?"

text.content:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["63", "79", "56", "I'd need to see the array"]
"If 9 × 7 = 63, what's 7 × 9?"

rich_text.plain_text:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["63", "79", "56", "I'd need to see the array"]
"If 9 × 7 = 63, what's 7 × 9?"

text.content: ✅ Student selected 63, correct application of property without visual support

rich_text.plain_text: ✅ Student selected 63, correct application of property without visual support

text.content: "Right, 63. You didn't need an array. You used the turn-around facts."

rich_text.plain_text: "Right, 63. You didn't need an array. You used the turn-around facts."

text.content: ❌ Student selected 79, treating the problem as concatenation rather than multiplication

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected 79, treating the problem as concatenation rather than multiplication

text.content: "Not quite. Here's a hint: think about turn-around facts. 9 times 7 equals 63. The turn-around fact means 7 times 9 equals the same product. Try again."

rich_text.plain_text: "Not quite. Here's a hint: think about turn-around facts. 9 times 7 equals 63. The turn-around fact means 7 times 9 equals the same product. Try again."

text.content: ❌ Student selected 56, likely computing 7×8 or misremembering the fact

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected 56, likely computing 7×8 or misremembering the fact

text.content: "Let's think about turn-around facts. We know 9 times 7 is 63. When we switch the factors around, we get the same product. What does 7 times 9 equal?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Let's think about turn-around facts. We know 9 times 7 is 63. When we switch the factors around, we get the same product. What does 7 times 9 equal?"

text.content: ❌ Student selected need to see array, not yet trusting the property without visual support

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected need to see array, not yet trusting the property without visual support

text.content: "Here's what helps: you've seen that rotating an array doesn't change the total dots. That means the turn-around facts work even when you can't see the array. If 9 times 7 equals 63, then 7 times 9 has the same product. What will it equal?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Here's what helps: you've seen that rotating an array doesn't change the total dots. That means the turn-around facts work even when you can't see the array. If 9 times 7 equals 63, then 7 times 9 has the same product. What will it equal?"

text.content: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

text.content: "Let's think through this. We know 9 times 7 equals 63. When we switch the factors, we get 7 times 9. The turn-around facts tell us this equals the same product: 63. You don't need an array because you know the turn-around facts."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let's think through this. We know 9 times 7 equals 63. When we switch the factors, we get 7 times 9. The turn-around facts tell us this equals the same product: 63. You don't need an array because you know the turn-around facts."

text.content: "Will this work for ANY two numbers you multiply, not just the ones we tried?"

rich_text.plain_text: "Will this work for ANY two numbers you multiply, not just the ones we tried?"

text.content:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["Yes, it always works", "Only for small numbers", "Only when you have an array", "Only when I know the first multiplication fact"]
"Does 'multiply factors in any order and get the same product' work for ALL numbers?"

rich_text.plain_text:
tool: multiple_choice  options: ["Yes, it always works", "Only for small numbers", "Only when you have an array", "Only when I know the first multiplication fact"]
"Does 'multiply factors in any order and get the same product' work for ALL numbers?"

text.content: ✅ Student selected always works, correct generalization to universal principle

rich_text.plain_text: ✅ Student selected always works, correct generalization to universal principle

text.content: "Exactly. Any two numbers. You could build an array for 9 × 7 and rotate it. Same dots, same product. You can imagine an array even when you don't see one. That's why mathematicians call it a property. It's always true."

rich_text.plain_text: "Exactly. Any two numbers. You could build an array for 9 × 7 and rotate it. Same dots, same product. You can imagine an array even when you don't see one. That's why mathematicians call it a property. It's always true."

text.content: ❌ Student selected only for small numbers, limiting the property to a subset of numbers

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected only for small numbers, limiting the property to a subset of numbers

text.content: "You're working on it. The key is that you could imagine an array for ANY two numbers. Whether it's 3 times 4 or 99 times 47, rotating the array always gives you the same dots. The property works for all numbers, not just small ones."

rich_text.plain_text: "You're working on it. The key is that you could imagine an array for ANY two numbers. Whether it's 3 times 4 or 99 times 47, rotating the array always gives you the same dots. The property works for all numbers, not just small ones."

text.content: ❌ Student selected only when you have an array, not yet seeing property as independent of visualization

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected only when you have an array, not yet seeing property as independent of visualization

text.content: "Here's a hint: you can always imagine an array, even when you don't draw one. Whether you see 5 times 8 as rows and columns or not, switching the factors still gives you the same product. The property works even when you don't have an array in front of you."

rich_text.plain_text: "Here's a hint: you can always imagine an array, even when you don't draw one. Whether you see 5 times 8 as rows and columns or not, switching the factors still gives you the same product. The property works even when you don't have an array in front of you."

text.content: ❌ Student selected only when I know the first fact, missing that property is structural not memorization-dependent

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student selected only when I know the first fact, missing that property is structural not memorization-dependent

text.content: "Let's think about this a bit more. The turn-around facts aren't about what you've memorized. They're about how multiplication itself works. Any two numbers, whether you know the fact or not, will give you the same product when you switch them. That's what makes it a property."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let's think about this a bit more. The turn-around facts aren't about what you've memorized. They're about how multiplication itself works. Any two numbers, whether you know the fact or not, will give you the same product when you switch them. That's what makes it a property."

text.content: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

rich_text.plain_text: ❌ Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

text.content: "Let me walk you through this one. Imagine any two numbers, like 9 and 7. You could build an array with 9 rows and 7 columns. Now rotate it: 7 rows and 9 columns. Same dots, same product. This works for ANY two numbers, big or small, whether you see an array or just imagine one. That's why it's called a property. It's always true. It always works to multiply the factors in any order and get the same product."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let me walk you through this one. Imagine any two numbers, like 9 and 7. You could build an array with 9 rows and 7 columns. Now rotate it: 7 rows and 9 columns. Same dots, same product. This works for ANY two numbers, big or small, whether you see an array or just imagine one. That's why it's called a property. It's always true. It always works to multiply the factors in any order and get the same product."

text.content: "This isn't true for every operation. You can't always change the order. 8 minus 3 is NOT the same as 3 minus 8. But with multiplication, you can always switch the factors and get the same product. You already knew addition worked this way: 3 plus 5 equals 5 plus 3. Now you've proven multiplication works the same way."

rich_text.plain_text: "This isn't true for every operation. You can't always change the order. 8 minus 3 is NOT the same as 3 minus 8. But with multiplication, you can always switch the factors and get the same product. You already knew addition worked this way: 3 plus 5 equals 5 plus 3. Now you've proven multiplication works the same way."

text.content: 3.1  Strategy Value Every Fact Has Partner  [s3_1_strategy_value_every_fact_has_partner]

rich_text.plain_text: 3.1  Strategy Value Every Fact Has Partner  [s3_1_strategy_value_every_fact_has_partner]

text.content: Display showing three multiplication facts with their commutative partners revealed: 3 × 8 = 24 paired with 8 × 3 = 24 (checkmark), 6 × 5 = 30 paired with 5 × 6 = 30 (checkmark), 4 × 9 = 36 paired with 9 × 4 = 36 (checkmark). Below the facts: text reads '3 facts learned → 6 facts known'.

rich_text.plain_text: Display showing three multiplication facts with their commutative partners revealed: 3 × 8 = 24 paired with 8 × 3 = 24 (checkmark), 6 × 5 = 30 paired with 5 × 6 = 30 (checkmark), 4 × 9 = 36 paired with 9 × 4 = 36 (checkmark). Below the facts: text reads '3 facts learned → 6 facts known'.

text.content: "Look at this. You learned 3 facts. But because you can multiply the factors in any order, you actually know 6 facts. Every fact gives you its partner for free."

rich_text.plain_text: "Look at this. You learned 3 facts. But because you can multiply the factors in any order, you actually know 6 facts. Every fact gives you its partner for free."

tool: multiple_choice  options: ["If I know 5 × 7, I already know 7 × 5, no extra work", "If I know 5 × 7, I should still compute 7 × 5 separately to be sure", "The strategy only helps when I have an array in front of me"]

"Which is a good use of the strategy?"

rich_text.plain_text: ✅ Student selected option A, recognizing the property as a time-saving strategy

text.content: "That's it. No extra work. One fact, two answers. That's what makes this a strategy, it saves you time and effort."

rich_text.plain_text: "That's it. No extra work. One fact, two answers. That's what makes this a strategy, it saves you time and effort."

text.content: "You're working on it. The key is that you don't NEED to compute both. Look at 3 × 8 and 8 × 3. They are turn-around facts. They give the same product. One fact tells you both answers."

rich_text.plain_text: "You're working on it. The key is that you don't NEED to compute both. Look at 3 × 8 and 8 × 3. They are turn-around facts. They give the same product. One fact tells you both answers."

text.content: ❌ Student selected option C: limiting strategy to visual contexts

text.content: "Let's think about this together. The commutative property works with any multiplication, not just arrays. Whether you see a picture or just numbers, 5 × 7 and 7 × 5 both equal 35."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let's think about this together. The commutative property works with any multiplication, not just arrays. Whether you see a picture or just numbers, 5 × 7 and 7 × 5 both equal 35."

text.content: Option A highlights: 'If I know 5 × 7, I already know 7 × 5 — no extra work'.

rich_text.plain_text: Option A highlights: 'If I know 5 × 7, I already know 7 × 5 — no extra work'.

text.content: "Let me explain. The strategy is about saving work. When you know 5 × 7 equals 35, you automatically know 7 × 5 equals 35 too. No extra computing needed. That is what makes it a strategy, it lets you skip work you would otherwise have to do."

rich_text.plain_text: "Let me explain. The strategy is about saving work. When you know 5 × 7 equals 35, you automatically know 7 × 5 equals 35 too. No extra computing needed. That is what makes it a strategy, it lets you skip work you would otherwise have to do."

text.content: 4.1  Key Takeaway Metacognitive  [s4_1_key_takeaway_metacognitive]

rich_text.plain_text: 4.1  Key Takeaway Metacognitive  [s4_1_key_takeaway_metacognitive]

text.content: "If you had to remember ONE thing from today, what would it be?"

tool: multiple_choice  options: ["You can multiply in any order and always get the same product", "Arrays can be read by rows or columns", "Rotation proves that rows become columns", "Knowing one multiplication fact gives you its partner"]

tool: multiple_choice  options: ["You can multiply in any order and always get the same product", "Arrays can be read by rows or columns", "Rotation proves that rows become columns", "Knowing one multiplication fact gives you its partner"]

text.content: "Think about where you started. You found equal groups in bags and circles. You learned that multiplication means groups of the same size. You built expressions, then equations. You discovered arrays: organized groups with rows and columns. And today, you proved something powerful: you can multiply in any order and always get the same answer."

rich_text.plain_text: "Think about where you started. You found equal groups in bags and circles. You learned that multiplication means groups of the same size. You built expressions, then equations. You discovered arrays: organized groups with rows and columns. And today, you proved something powerful: you can multiply in any order and always get the same answer."
